headlines.title: Driver arrested after car crashes into San Jose home
headlines.description: A driver was arrested after a car crashed into a home Tuesday night in San Jose. Officers responded to the 900 block of North 17th Street on a report of a vehicle into a house. When they arrived, they learned the vehicle had collided with a light pole before striking the home’s carport. Two juveniles who were in the backyard at the time suffered non-life-threatening injuries. Police said the adult male driver was believed to be under the influence. He was taken to a local hospital for evaluation and later booked into the Santa Clara County jail on suspicion of DUI.

headlines.title: Overturned big rig blocked northbound Highway 101 lanes in Gilroy
headlines.description: An overturned semi-truck blocked lanes Wednesday morning on northbound Highway 101 in Gilroy. At least two lanes were closed after the truck, which was carrying asphalt, overturned just before midnight. The cause of the crash is under investigation. No injuries were reported. The truck has been lifted upright, and all lanes have since reopened.

headlines.title: Vigil held at SFSU for human rights activist killed in Philippines
headlines.description: Friends, family and former classmates are mourning the death of San Francisco State University alum Lyle Prijoles, a human rights activist killed alongside 18 others in the Philippines last month. A community vigil was held Tuesday night at SFSU to honor Prijoles and the other victims. Pete Suratos reports from the university. Watch his story in the video above.

headlines.title: War concerns derail trip, North Bay woman struggles to get refund
headlines.description: A North Bay woman’s vacation plans were derailed by concerns over conflict in the Middle East — and now she’s fighting to get her money back. Leslie Russo, an accountant, typically takes two trips a year. Her spring trip to South Korea went smoothly, but a planned fall trip to Egypt hit a roadblock. Russo booked a 10-day sightseeing tour through a South Lake Tahoe-based group, Snow More Ski and Board Club, and paid about $3,900 in three deposits. “And then this war broke out in the Middle East and I was getting a little nervous,” Russo said. “And I decided I’m going to cancel the trip.” Travel experts note that during times of conflict, airlines and travel providers often offer added flexibility. “There has been a general consensus that airlines, hotels and travel providers are trying to be as accommodating as possible,” said Katy Nastro of Going.com, describing wartime waivers that can allow changes or refunds without penalties. She calls the waivers a “golden ticket.” “We like to call it a golden ticket scenario because it’s a Charlie and the Chocolate Factory moment. You have the keys to make changes.” Russo received a full refund for her flight within days. But canceling the land portion of her Egypt trip proved more difficult. Her contract listed a $300 cancellation fee if she canceled within 159 days of the trip. Russo canceled more than 200 days in advance and expected a full refund. Instead, the travel group withheld $1,900. NBC Bay Area’s Chris Chmura has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: SFMTA beefs up efforts to crackdown on Muni fare evaders
headlines.description: San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency plans to crack down on Muni fare evaders by bringing in more inspectors. The transit agency plans to add 17 more employees to its team of 59 fare inspectors. “I want to say the average is about five to seven per shift,” SFMTA fare inspector V. Lujan said of how many passengers they catch daily who have have not paid to ride Muni. “It depends on the line. It depends on the time.” SFMTA figures show an increase of about $14 million in rider revenue compared to last year. But the agency knows there are still fare cheats taking advantage of the system. San Francisco Apr 22 San Francisco Feb 3, 2025 San Francisco Jun 25, 2025 Muni estimates that about one in every four of its riders are not paying their fare share. One of the problems SFMTA currently has is tracking how many people are legally riding for free. During a news event at the Castro neighborhood Muni station, which included Mayor Daniel Lurie, Muni leaders announced a new effort to sort out riders from cheats. The plan includes asking those who are legally riding for free to start tapping a smart phone or pass so the agency can get better tracking info. “One of the benefits of this initiative is getting more people tapping, which will give us better data,” said Julie Kirshbaum, SFMTA director of transportation. “We estimate about half of our customers pay in an invisible way like through an app or are free like our youth.” Anyone 18 or younger can currently ride Muni free of charge. Board of Supervisors President Rafael Mandelman is cheering the new effort to crackdown on fare evaders. “Because of our great fare inspectors, when you get on that train or bus, you are going to know that those people are paying,” Mandelman said. Riders who spoke to NBC Bay Area on Tuesday said uniformed fare inspectors on buses and trains also make them feel a little safer on their commutes.

headlines.title: OpenAI co-founder says he feared Musk would ‘physically attack' him
headlines.description: The internal debate at tech startup OpenAI got so heated back in 2017 that one of its co-founders, Greg Brockman, feared that co-founder Elon Musk would hit him, Brockman testified Tuesday in a trial that will determine the future of the organization behind ChatGPT. “I truly thought he was going to physically attack me,” Brockman said during the fifth day of the trial. Brockman said the tense encounter happened in August that year, shortly after the OpenAI co-founders decided they needed far more money to develop artificial intelligence than they thought they’d be able to raise as a charity. He testified that they were batting around ideas, including the creation of a for-profit arm. “The meeting started out very pleasant,” Brockman testified. Musk was there and had just given Tesla vehicles as gifts to his fellow OpenAI co-founders, Brockman said. Musk is the longtime CEO of Tesla and SpaceX and, years later, would found his own AI startup, xAI, which competes with OpenAI. Ilya Sutskever, another of the co-founders, reciprocated at the meeting by giving Musk a Tesla-related painting as a gift. But Brockman testified that the tone of the meeting changed when the topic of equity shares in the proposed for-profit OpenAI arm came up. He said that Musk wanted majority control of the organization and rejected a proposal that all the co-founders get equal shares. “He said, ‘I decline,’” Brockman said. He said Musk refused to accept others having a say. “He said, ‘When are you going to be departing OpenAI?’ He said, ‘I will withhold funding until you decide what to do.’” By then, he said, Musk was visibly angry and he stood up, stormed around the room, grabbed the painting and left. No one ended up exchanging blows, he said. Elon Musk Apr 29 Artificial intelligence Apr 27 Artificial intelligence Apr 26 The meeting was part of a chaotic period in OpenAI’s history that may be central to the trial. Musk is suing Brockman and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, alleging they breached the duty they owed OpenAI as a charity and have enriched themselves. Brockman and Altman counter that OpenAI is still controlled by a nonprofit foundation and that Musk agreed with them years ago about the need to raise money from investors in order to attract and retain the best AI researchers. Testimony began last week with Musk, who said that Brockman and Altman misled him about their intentions as they gradually made changes at OpenAI before he sued in 2024. Musk testified that he made a $38 million founding donation to OpenAI. In 2024, OpenAI emphasized that it had raised $90 million from sources besides Musk. OpenAI was founded in 2015. It added a capped-profit arm in 2019 and announced a restructuring last year that resulted in the nonprofit foundation having a 26% ownership share. If the jury and judge find Altman and Brockman liable, Musk has said he wants the court to bar them from working at OpenAI and order other changes to undo its restructuring. Brockman testified for four hours Tuesday and described other unusual moments from OpenAI’s early days. Just weeks before the meeting where Musk became angry, Brockman said there was a meeting at a San Francisco-area house that Musk owned and called his “haunted mansion” because of its dated decor and lack of maintenance. Musk warned his co-founders ahead of that meeting to expect “party carnage” in the house, Brockman testified. “It was clear that there had been a party there the night before. There was confetti and cups all around,” Brockman said. That meeting also included two women who had or would later have personal connections to Musk, Brockman said: actor Amber Heard, whom Musk was dating at the time, and Musk adviser Shivon Zilis, who now has several children with Musk. Zilis is expected to testify at the trial on Wednesday. She has worked for Musk’s brain science startup, Neuralink, and served on the OpenAI board of directors until 2023, operating as a bridge between the AI firm and Musk after he had earlier left the board. On the stand Tuesday, Brockman said that although he admired some parts of Musk’s business record, he became disillusioned with the billionaire’s understanding of AI. He said Musk appeared underwhelmed when presented with GPT-1, an early version of the AI technology that would later underpin ChatGPT. Musk called the technology “stupid” and said “kids on the internet” could do a better job, statements that dismayed an early OpenAI employee who nearly left the industry over the incident, Brockman testified. “He knows rockets, he knows electric cars, and I believe he did not — and does not — know AI,” Brockman said, adding, “I did not believe he would spend enough time to get good at it.” He said that he and fellow co-founders “considered voting to remove Elon from the board” in 2017 before deciding against the idea. He left the board voluntarily in 2018. Brockman faced numerous questions about a journal that he kept on his laptop to document and think through both personal and business matters. In one entry from September 2017 that has repeatedly come up during the trial, Brockman wrote about his desire to “get out from Elon” and wondered, “Financially, what will take me to $1B?” Brockman testified that it was “very painful” to have the journal quoted in open court but added, “There’s nothing in there I’m ashamed of.” He said in testimony a day earlier that his stake in OpenAI is now worth nearly $30 billion, a figure that would make him among the wealthiest 100 people in the world, according to Bloomberg. U.S. District Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers told jurors Tuesday that evidence in the case could wrap up early next week, clearing them to begin deliberating. She said some jurors had been submitting questions to her about the case. “I have been receiving your questions and I have been sharing them with the lawyers. The lawyers have been trying to incorporate those questions into their examinations,” the judge said, though she did not reveal the contents of the questions.

headlines.title: Vandals rip crosses out, desecrate memorials at Oakland church
headlines.description: An Oakland church is trying to understand why vandals this week ripped out nearly every cross, desecrated a memorial and toppled over a statue. St. Columbia Catholic Church is known for recognizing homicide victims and taking stands on controversial issues. Aidan McAleenan, the priest of the parish, made the discover after Tuesday morning’s mass. Most of the crosses had been ripped out, broken and thrown in the street. The vandals also destroyed a fountain made of six standing stones in honor of six Irish college students who died when a balcony collapsed in Berkeley in 2015. A statue of the Virgin de Guadalupe was also toppled by the vandals, McAleenan said, adding some potted plants were also found broken. McAleenan said he is not sure why someone would target the church. The church has signs displayed calling for a ceasefire in Gaza, promoting Black Lives Matter and warning ICE it cannot enter without a warrant. McAleenan said he is not certain if any of the church’s stances on the issues angered someone, but he said St. Columbia is inclusive and works hard to serve and welcome everyone. McAleenan said he will not let the vandals break their spirit and the church will continue to serve people, love people and see people. Oakland May 1 Oakland May 1 Oakland May 2 Oakland's Mosswood Recreation Center opens its doors after 10 years

headlines.title: Several cases of Legionella at Santa Clara hospital
headlines.description: Kaiser Permanente Santa Clara Medical Center is dealing with several cases of Legionella, a highly contagious bacteria that can lead to pneumonia. As the hospital works to maintain a safe environment for patients and staff, it’s still trying to find the source. “Legionella loves warm water,” UCSF infectious disease expert Dr. Peter Chin-Hong said. “You breathe it in through droplets, usually from faulty air conditioning or hot tub or fountain.” Experts said those most vulnerable are the elderly, people who smoke and those with auto-immune deficiencies. Legionnaires’ disease can be fatal if ignored, but it is treatable with specific antibiotics. “You might have some nausea, vomiting with that,” Chin-Hong said. “And second – they are warning signs you need to go to the hospital – that might be extreme shortness of breath and abnormal fever.” In a statement, Kaiser Permanente said, in part, “Out of an abundance of caution we have implemented additional water treatment measures and have taken preventive measures to ensure the safety of all patients, employees, and visitors.” There are no alerts at hospital entrances, and operations remain normal. Kaiser Permanente said it is working closely with local and state public health authorities to mitigate the issue. The hospital said it discovered the issue through its robust internal monitoring process. Read Kaiser Permanente’s full statement below: “Kaiser Permanente Santa Clara recently confirmed several cases of Legionella infection. The source of the contamination is unclear at this time. Legionella bacteria occur naturally in water and are spread by inhaling contaminated mist, not through person-to-person contact. Our experts are working closely with local and state public health authorities. The bacteria was identified through our robust routine internal monitoring processes, which is in compliance with all regulatory standards. While we work to determine the source, out of an abundance of caution we have implemented additional water treatment measures and have taken preventive measures to ensure the safety of all patients, employees, and visitors. As a result of mitigation efforts, our hospital and medical office building are operating normally and continuing to care safely for patients. We take this matter seriously and are committed to maintaining a safe environment for all patients, members, visitors, and staff.”

headlines.title: Bay Area hotels not seeing a World Cup surge, report says
headlines.description: With just over a month until the World Cup, host cities are getting nervous because they aren’t seeing the hotel demand they originally expected. About 75% of them said bookings are tracking below initial forecasts. NBC Bay Area’s Marianne Favro has more in the video report above. 2026 World Cup Apr 29 2026 World Cup Mar 3 Everything to know about the 2026 FIFA World Cup: Teams, how to watch and more

headlines.title: 1 dead, 1 in custody after shooting in Antioch
headlines.description: A man is dead and a suspect is in custody after a shooting in Antioch on Tuesday morning, according to police. Officers responded at 9:14 a.m. to the shooting reported in the 5000 block of Percheron Way and arrived to find the victim, who was pronounced dead at the scene, Antioch police said. The suspect was located nearby and taken into custody after allegedly breaking into a residence unrelated to the shooting, and investigators recovered the weapon allegedly used in the killing, according to police. The names of the victim and suspect were not immediately available. Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact Detective Becerra at (925) 779-7937 or abecerra@antiochca.gov or the department’s Investigations Bureau at (925) 779-6926.

headlines.title: Alcatraz coyote likely swam from Angel Island, not San Francisco
headlines.description: The coyote that managed to swim to Alcatraz Island earlier this year likely made the epic journey from Angel Island State Park, not San Francisco as previously presumed, the National Park Service said Monday. Officials don’t know where the coyote is now, but they were able to match him to Angel Island’s coyote population thanks to a scat sample. That means the coyote likely accomplished a nearly 2-mile swim between the two islands instead of a roughly 1-mile journey from San Francisco. “We are surprised by the coyote’s origin,” National Park Service wildlife ecologist Bill Merkle said in a statement. “Our working assumption was that the coyote made the swim from San Francisco because it is a significantly shorter distance. We couldn’t help being impressed by his accomplishment in making it to Alcatraz. Coyotes are known to be resilient and adaptable, and he certainly demonstrated those qualities.” After the coyote was spotted on Alcatraz in late January, park biologists launched a mission to learn more about the animal. Staff searched the island for tracks, installed trail cameras and audio recording devices, and collected samples for lab testing, the park service said. Coyote scat that was collected on the island was submitted to UC Davis for DNA analysis. San Francisco Jan 28 San Francisco Jan 19 Dr. Ben Sacks at UC Davis said the Alcatraz coyote would have come from one of three distinct coyote populations: San Francisco, Southern Marin or Angel Island. “Our lab was able to take the DNA sample and match it to a coyote previously sampled from the Angel Island population,” Sacks said in a statement. Alcatraz staff spent the past several months keeping an eye out for the coyote in hopes of relocating it to a more suitable habitat, but the animal’s whereabouts are unknown at this time. No evidence points to the coyote still being on the island, and no remains have been found, the park service said. “We don’t know what happened to the coyote,” Merkle said in a statement. “But he proved himself an expert swimmer to get to Alcatraz, and I hope he made a successful swim back home to Angel Island.”

headlines.title: Alysa Liu, Eileen Gu, Steph Curry represent Bay Area at Met Gala
headlines.description: The Bay Area was well represented at the Met Gala on Monday night, with several high-profile athletes and celebrities making appearances at one of fashion’s biggest events. Oakland native and Olympic champion Alysa Liu made her Met Gala debut, wearing a red gown by Louis Vuitton. The luxury fashion house recently named Liu as a brand ambassador. Fellow Olympic gold medalist Eileen Gu also walked the carpet, drawing attention in a dress made of glass bubbles that produced real bubbles. Golden State Warriors star Steph Curry and his wife, Ayesha Curry, were also in attendance. The Met Gala is one of the most prominent nights in fashion, drawing celebrities from around the world. Met Gala May 5 May 4 Lindsey Vonn walks Met Gala steps without crutches for first time since Olympics crash
